title: Are India's courts ready to keep the promises this draft makes?
author: Harsh Gour
summary: The draft assumes an operational ecosystem — audits, secretariats, training — that many courts don't yet have.
body:
## Key critiques

- **Institutional capacity gap**: the draft assumes AI secretariats can run in-house audits, but many district courts are still building basic case-management software
- **Who governs the governors**: there is no external audit of the Apex Body; the self-review provision (Reg. 57) makes the SC AI Committee judge of its own decisions
- **Training ≠ literacy**: a curriculum is prescribed, but with no duration, no competency assessment, and no consequence for failure
- **Procedural compliance ≠ safety**: a court can satisfy every rule and still deploy harmful AI
- **No pattern detection**: incident logging is case-by-case; nothing mandates spotting systemic failures across multiple courts
- **Grievance gaps**: unrepresented district-court litigants have no clear notification path or investigation mechanism
- **Comparative note**: India's draft is more ambitious on paper than the UK, US, or Singapore — but weaker on the operational ecosystem underneath
